paragraphs.text: Student of the Jean Monnet Modules project “DERECHO”: ‘Defense of European Rights and Excellence for Consumers HHarmonisation’, No 101239437. Project financed by the European Union. Jean Monnet Actions in the field of Higher Education: Modules. Call for tenders ERASMUS-JMO-2025-HEI-TCH-RSCH. As an EU citizen and a third party law student, I believe it is crucial that up-to-date binding rules (and not just soft law) are established, imposing conditions primarily aimed at protecting consumers who, in many situations, face misleading advertising practices. In general, it is the users who are unprotected from the companies carrying out these practices, so that the responsibility should lie primarily with the relevant authorities, which have the means and power to provide them with the necessary protection. The protection of citizens must be exercised both actively and passively, that is to say, consumers must, by their own means, remain vigilant and seek to avoid content that is not of interest to them. However, at the same time, the responsible authorities should take the necessary steps to ensure that the information received by users is truthful, transparent and useful. In addition, they should provide adequate means for users themselves to be aware of the nature of that information, so that they are able to decide for themselves whether it is useful to them or not. If this is done by means of soft law, it would be more difficult to implement such measures, as they are not binding and therefore the authorities could avoid carrying out certain actions involving greater effort or higher cost. I therefore consider that new binding rules should be implemented to increase consumer protection, through greater protection by the responsible authorities.
